examples.title: Memory Read Operation Using Bus
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how CPU performs a memory read operation through the bus
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the step-by-step process of a CPU reading data from memory: placing the address on the address bus, activating the memory read signal, waiting for the memory to respond, and then reading the data from the data bus.
examples.timeComplexity: O(w) where w is the number of wait states

examples.title: Bus Arbitration Controller in C
examples.description: C code demonstrating independent request arbitration logic
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates independent request arbitration where multiple devices can request the bus simultaneously. The arbiter evaluates all requests and grants the bus to the device with the highest priority (lowest priority number).
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of devices
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing request and grant flags

examples.title: Bus Bandwidth Calculator
examples.description: Python program to calculate bus bandwidth and analyze performance
examples.code:
def calculate_bandwidth(data_bus_width_bits, clock_frequency_mhz, transfer_efficiency=1.0):
    """
    Calculate bus bandwidth
    
    Args:
        data_bus_width_bits: Width of data bus in bits
        clock_frequency_mhz: Clock frequency in MHz
        transfer_efficiency: Efficiency factor (0.0 to 1.0)
    
    Returns:
        Bandwidth in MB/s
    """
    # Convert bits to bytes
    data_bus_width_bytes = data_bus_width_bits // 8
    
    # Calculate bandwidth: bytes per transfer × transfers per second
    bandwidth_mb_per_s = (data_bus_width_bytes * clock_frequency_mhz * transfer_efficiency)
    
    return bandwidth_mb_per_s

def compare_bus_configurations():
    """Compare different bus configurations"""
    configurations = [
        {"name": "8-bit ISA Bus", "width": 8, "freq": 8.33, "efficiency": 0.9},
        {"name": "16-bit ISA Bus", "width": 16, "freq": 8.33, "efficiency": 0.9},
        {"name": "32-bit PCI Bus", "width": 32, "freq": 33, "efficiency": 0.95},
        {"name": "64-bit PCI-X Bus", "width": 64, "freq": 133, "efficiency": 0.95},
        {"name": "64-bit System Bus", "width": 64, "freq": 400, "efficiency": 0.98},
    ]
    
    print("Bus Configuration Comparison")
    print("=" * 60)
    
    for config in configurations:
        bw = calculate_bandwidth(
            config["width"], 
            config["freq"], 
            config["efficiency"]
        )
        print(f"{config['name']:20s} | {bw:8.2f} MB/s")

# Example calculations
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Calculate bandwidth for 32-bit bus at 100 MHz
    bw = calculate_bandwidth(32, 100)
    print(f"32-bit bus at 100 MHz: {bw:.2f} MB/s\n")
    
    # Compare configurations
    compare_bus_configurations()
examples.explanation: This Python program calculates bus bandwidth using the formula: Bandwidth = (Data Bus Width / 8) × Clock Frequency × Efficiency. It demonstrates how bus width and clock frequency affect data transfer rates.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of configurations

examples.title: Daisy Chain Bus Arbitration
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating daisy chain arbitration mechanism
examples.code:
// Daisy Chain Arbitration Algorithm

CONSTANT NUM_DEVICES = 4

// Device structure
STRUCTURE Device
    id: INTEGER
    priority: INTEGER  // Higher value = higher priority
    request_line: BOOLEAN
    grant_line: BOOLEAN
ENDSTRUCTURE

// Initialize devices in priority order (device 0 = highest priority)
ARRAY devices[NUM_DEVICES] WITH Device

PROCEDURE daisy_chain_arbiter()
BEGIN
    BOOLEAN grant_propagate ← TRUE
    
    // Process devices in priority order (highest first)
    FOR i ← 0 TO NUM_DEVICES - 1 DO
        IF devices[i].request_line AND grant_propagate THEN
            // Grant bus to this device
            devices[i].grant_line ← TRUE
            grant_propagate ← FALSE
            PRINT "Bus granted to Device " + devices[i].id
        ELSE
            // Pass grant to next device in chain
            devices[i].grant_line ← FALSE
        END IF
    END FOR
END PROCEDURE

// Simulate device requesting bus
PROCEDURE device_request(device_id, request)
BEGIN
    devices[device_id].request_line ← request
    IF request THEN
        PRINT "Device " + device_id + " requests bus"
    END IF
END PROCEDURE

// Example execution
BEGIN
    // Setup device priorities
    devices[0].id ← 0
    devices[1].id ← 1
    devices[2].id ← 2
    devices[3].id ← 3
    
    // Devices request bus
    device_request(2, TRUE)  // Device 2 requests
    device_request(3, TRUE)  // Device 3 requests
    
    // Run arbitration
    daisy_chain_arbiter()
    // Device 2 (higher priority) gets the bus
END
examples.explanation: In daisy chain arbitration, devices are connected in series. The grant signal propagates through the chain, and the first requesting device (highest priority) captures the grant signal, blocking it from reaching lower priority devices.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of devices in chain
